meta.title: Beam Deflection Calculator | Structural Theory Guide | AluCalc
meta.description: Calculate beam deflection using Euler-Bernoulli theory. Professional guide on moments of inertia, boundary conditions, and structural limits.

seo.h1: Structural Mechanics: Theory of Beam Deflection
seo.intro: Beam deflection analysis is fundamental to structural engineering, ensuring that members have sufficient stiffness to maintain functional integrity under load. This guide explores the Euler-Bernoulli beam theory and the mathematical derivation of deflection curves for industrial spans.
seo.practical: Used to verify architectural floor joists, industrial overhead cranes, machine tool beds, and bridge spans for compliance with L/360 or L/500 deflection limits.
seo.example: Scenario: A 4-meter simply supported steel beam (I = 1,000 cm⁴) with a 5,000 N center load: δ = (5000 * 4³) / (48 * 210e9 * 1e-5) ≈ 0.00317m (3.17 mm).
seo.step_by_step:
1. Identify the Beam Material and its Young's Modulus (E).
2. Calculate the Area Moment of Inertia (I) for the beam cross-section.
3. Determine the Load Magnitude (P) and Span Length (L).
4. Select the formula corresponding to your Boundary Conditions (e.g., Cantilever vs. Simply Supported).

technicalSections.title: Euler-Bernoulli Beam Theory
content: The Euler-Bernoulli theory assumes that 'plane sections remain plane'. This means that during bending, the cross-section of the beam stays perpendicular to the neutral axis and does not warp.

This linear approximation is highly accurate for 'slender' beams where the length-to-depth ratio exceeds 10:1. For shorter, deep beams, Timoshenko theory (which includes shear deformation) is preferred.

technicalSections.title: Influence of Area Moment of Inertia (I)
content: The Area Moment of Inertia (I) defines the geometric stiffness of a section. Doubling the height of a rectangular beam increases its stiffness by a factor of 8 (cubed relationship), while doubling the width only doubles the stiffness.

technicalSections.title: Standard Boundary Conditions
content: The maximum deflection is highly sensitive to how the beam is supported. A cantilever beam (fixed at one end) deflects 16 times more than a simply supported beam of the same length and load.
